title: The Year the Letters Stopped
bullets: Around 410 Roman imperial authority dissolved in Britain, ending four centuries of written administration, tax records, and paid armies in what was one of the most thoroughly Romanized societies on the empire's northern frontier.

Emperor Honorius reportedly sent a letter to British cities telling them to see to their own defense — the Rescript of Honorius — though modern historians dispute whether it even refers to Britain at all.
The Britons, Celtic-speaking descendants of Roman citizens, had their identity stripped away as coin shipments dried up and the field army was withdrawn to fight civil wars on the Continent.

The theologian Pelagius, born in Britain around 400, sparked a continent-wide heresy controversy by arguing humans could earn salvation through free will, and post-Roman Britain kept entertaining his condemned ideas for decades.
In 429 the Gallic bishop Germanus of Auxerre crossed the Channel to argue Britons out of Pelagianism and reportedly led a bloodless Hallelujah Victory over Pictish and Saxon raiders.
Britain faced simultaneous threats from three directions: Picts raiding from the north, Gaelic Scotti from Ireland in the west, and Germanic Saxons from across the North Sea in the east.

Rome had already anticipated the Saxon threat by building the Saxon Shore forts, a chain of massive coastal defenses from the Wash to the Solent — physical proof these raiders were an expected danger before the empire left.

title: The Adventus Saxonum
bullets: The only substantial near-contemporary account of this period is Gildas writing in the 530s or 540s — a furious British monk composing a jeremiad against his own kings, vague about dates and allergic to naming anyone.

A British ruler Gildas calls a proud tyrant, later named Vortigern, hired Germanic warriors to fight the Picts, triggering the same fatal pattern seen across the Germanic world: the hired help became the landlord.

The historian Bede, writing around 731, gave the settlement its familiar shape: brothers Hengist and Horsa leading Angles, Saxons, and Jutes in three peoples who each took different regions of Britain.

A genuine migration debate divides historians: ancient-DNA evidence now suggests substantial North Sea migration that thins toward the west, combined with large-scale cultural assimilation of existing Britons over generations.

Brittonic, the Celtic language of the Britons, retreated into Wales, Cornwall, and Cumbria as Germanic speech replaced it across the lowlands — a language substitution unusual enough to suggest migration was real and substantial.

The Romano-British war leader Ambrosius Aurelianus rallied British resistance and his forces won a major victory at the Battle of Mount Badon around 500, checking the Saxon advance for perhaps a generation.

The name Arthur first appears attached to Badon centuries after the fact, never in any near-contemporary source — the legend is real, the historical kernel is a guess, and Gildas never mentions him at all.

title: The Kingdoms Form
bullets: There was never a single Anglo-Saxon conquest; instead warbands became chieftaincies became small kingdoms that spent more energy fighting each other than fighting the Britons, and the map of England is the fossil of which ones won.
Every Anglo-Saxon kingdom traced its royal genealogy back to the god Woden, and an early kingdom was essentially a successful warband that had put down roots and started claiming divine descent.

The two main sources — Bede writing in 731 and the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle begun under King Alfred in the late ninth century — both have strong agendas that must be held in mind when reading their foundation stories.

Kent, the corner closest to Frankish Gaul, emerged first and richest, its elite buried with garnet-inlaid gold and imported glassware, its dynasty uniquely positioned to receive Augustine's mission in Chapter 5.

Wessex's founder Cerdic has a Brittonic rather than Germanic name, hinting the earliest West Saxon elite was ethnically mixed — a detail the Chronicle, written to celebrate this dynasty, conspicuously fails to explain.
The Angles dominated north of the Thames: East Anglia under the Wuffingas, and the future Northumbria forming from the merger of two kingdoms, Bernicia and Deira, along the northeast coast.
Mercia, the kingdom whose name means borderland, emerged landlocked in the midlands on a violent frontier with the Welsh, a geography that made its kings aggressive and militarized by necessity.

title: The Heptarchy and the Buried King
bullets: The later label Heptarchy — rule of seven — is a schoolbook simplification: the real political map had scores of peoples, the Tribal Hidage listing dozens of tiny tributary groups alongside the famous kingdoms.
Bede identified a series of overking Bretwaldas who held imperium over multiple kingdoms, not a formal office but battlefield dominance acknowledged by tribute and hostages while it lasted.

Æthelberht of Kent, the first Bretwalda who is more than a name, married Bertha, a Frankish Christian princess, who came with her own bishop and worshipped in Canterbury for years before Augustine arrived.

Rædwald of East Anglia, the Bretwalda of his generation around 616, hedged his religion by keeping a Christian altar and a pagan altar in the same temple — Bede disapproved; history finds it endearing.

In 1939 a burial mound at Sutton Hoo in Suffolk was opened to reveal a 90-foot ship containing the richest assemblage of early medieval treasure ever found in Britain, almost certainly the grave of Rædwald's dynasty.

The Sutton Hoo helmet — iron war-mask with gilded boar eyebrows and a dragon across the crown — became the face of an era; the ship burial connects East Anglia directly to Scandinavian ship-funeral traditions at Vendel and Valsgärde.

Among Sutton Hoo's silver were two spoons inscribed Saulos and Paulos — Saul renamed Paul at his conversion — a possible baptismal gift buried in an overwhelmingly pagan context, catching the exact religious hinge of the age.

title: The Mission
bullets: In 597 Pope Gregory I sent the Roman monk Augustine with about forty companions to convert the English, landing in Kent because Bertha's earlier presence had already prepared the ground.

Gregory's famous Non Angli sed angeli — not Angles but angels — is a Latin pun too perfect to fully trust, but what is solid is that he organized the mission and aimed it at the leading English kingdom.

Conversion in early medieval England worked top-down through lordship: when a king accepted baptism, his nobles followed, but when that king was killed by a pagan rival the people often reverted with him.

Augustine founded the archbishopric of Canterbury in 597, the institution that has headed the English church for fourteen centuries, orienting England's spiritual center toward Rome and the Continent.

The Law Code of Æthelberht, written around 600, is the earliest surviving document in English and the earliest Germanic law written in a vernacular language rather than Latin — England's first act of writing was its own law.

Ireland had already developed its own intense monastic Christianity, and by 635 the Irish monk Aidan had founded Lindisfarne monastery in Northumbria — meaning England was being converted from two directions at once.

Cædmon, an illiterate herdsman at Whitby who dreamed he could sing of Creation, composed the earliest Old English poem we can name — Cædmon's Hymn — pouring Christian content into the pre-existing Germanic oral tradition.

title: Whitby and the Shape of the Church
bullets: Easter was not a fixed calendar date but required mathematical calculation, and Roman and Irish churches used different tables, so in many years the king's Easter feast and his queen's Lenten fast fell on the same day.

Penda of Mercia, the last great pagan king of England, spent decades killing Christian rivals including Edwin of Northumbria in 633 and Oswald in 642, proving the conversion's outcome was contingent on battle.

The Battle of the River Winwæd in 655 — Oswiu of Northumbria against Penda's much larger army, in flood — ended with Penda's death and permanently ended serious pagan competition for English kingship.

The Synod of Whitby in 664 was convened in the double monastery run by the royal abbess Hilda, herself one of the most powerful people in the English church, who had educated five future bishops in her house.

Wilfrid, championing Rome, argued that the keys of heaven were given to Peter, not to the Irish founding saint Colum Cille, and Oswiu ruled for Rome rather than risk arguing with the keeper of heaven's gate.

Theodore of Tarsus, a Greek-speaking monk from southern Turkey appointed Archbishop of Canterbury in 668, toured the whole of England, divided it into territorial dioceses, and in 672 convened the first all-English church council at Hertford.

Theodore and his North African companion Hadrian made the Canterbury school one of the only places in seventh-century Western Europe where students learned both Latin and Greek, along with law, natural science, and calendar mathematics.

title: The Northumbrian Golden Age
bullets: Benedict Biscop made at least five journeys to Rome, returning each time with books, glaziers, masons, and panel paintings, and used royal land grants to found the twin monastery of Wearmouth-Jarrow with a library of unprecedented size for northern Europe.

Bede was given to the monastery as a seven-year-old and never left it, writing from inside that single institution the Ecclesiastical History of the English People in 731, the foundational narrative of Anglo-Saxon England and one of the finest works of medieval historical method.

Bede did not merely tell stories about the past; his Reckoning of Time became the standard textbook on calendar science in Europe for centuries, and his observations of North Sea tidal patterns are genuine original science.
Bede's use of Anno Domini dating — years counted from the birth of Christ — popularized a system a Roman monk had devised but largely ignored, which is why the world now writes plain year numbers.

Insular manuscript art fused Celtic interlace, Germanic animal ornament, and Mediterranean Christian iconography into something no single tradition could have produced, with the Lindisfarne Gospels of around 715 as its supreme achievement.

The Codex Amiatinus, made at Wearmouth-Jarrow around 716, was the oldest complete Latin Bible and so convincingly Mediterranean in style that scholars assumed it was Italian until realizing it had been made in Northumbria.

Northumbrian monks Willibrord and Boniface crossed back over the North Sea to convert the pagan Germanic peoples of the Continent, reversing the original direction of the migration and making the student the teacher of its own ancestors.

title: The Mercian Supremacy
bullets: Æthelbald of Mercia ruled for over forty years from 716, styling himself king of all the southern English and backed by a sophisticated charter-producing court that made Mercian overlordship administrative, not just military.

Bookland, land conveyed by written charter in perpetuity rather than held by custom and conditional service, transformed the church's property rights and the land market, both of which now ran partly on writing.
Offa of Mercia, ruling 757 to 796, built Offa's Dyke — an earthwork frontier running roughly 80 miles along the Welsh border, the largest single engineering project of early medieval Britain.

Offa reformed English coinage with a standardized silver penny, setting the pattern for English money for five hundred years and asserting a state-level control over currency that a warband never could.

Offa dealt directly with Charlemagne, negotiating the first recorded commercial treaty between an English king and a Continental power, while simultaneously having the king of East Anglia beheaded in 794 for acting too independently.

Offa persuaded Pope Hadrian I in 787 to elevate Lichfield to an archbishopric, temporarily splitting the southern English church away from Canterbury — a maneuver by patronage and diplomacy that collapsed within twenty years of his death.

When Offa died in 796 his son survived him by only months, and the vast Mercian overlordship he had built had no institutional existence apart from the man — personal power, not a state, exactly as it had always been.

title: How the English Lived
bullets: The witan, an informal council of great men — bishops, ealdormen, senior nobles — gave royal acts their legitimacy and effectively chose each new king from among throne-worthy members of the dynasty, which is why so many successions were contested and bloody.

Ealdormen administered shires from local halls in an age with no permanent capital and no salaried civil service; the king himself travelled a circuit of royal estates, consuming food-renders brought by local people as a tax in kind.

Wergild, the man-price, was Anglo-Saxon law's load-bearing institution: a fixed monetary value on every free person's life scaled precisely to rank, converting what would otherwise be endless blood-feuds into calculable debts.

The ceorl — free farmer, ancestor of the insult churl — was the majority of the English population, while the thegn held land by military service and the wergild table valued a thegn's life at the price of several ceorls.

The open-field system divided cultivated land around each village into strips scattered across shared unfenced fields, stamping the English landscape with settlement names ending in -ham, -ton, and -ley that survive on maps today.

The wic trading emporia — Hamwic near Southampton, Lundenwic west of Roman London, Ipswich in East Anglia — were undefended planned coastal towns that boomed from the late seventh century on North Sea trade in wool, pottery, and slaves.

Beowulf, the great Old English epic composed in England by Christians, is set entirely in Scandinavia among Danes and Geats, the same ship-funeral world as Sutton Hoo, because the English still carried the homeland in their imagination three centuries after crossing the sea.

title: The Eve of the Storm
bullets: Around 789 a royal reeve named Beaduheard rode down to the Dorset coast near Portland to direct what he assumed were foreign traders to the king's estate for assessment — the men from three Norse ships killed him.
The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle records this as the first arrival of the ships of the Northmen, though the note was written a century later with hindsight the reeve did not have.

The Scandinavians had developed the true sailing ship by the late eighth century: a long, shallow, flexible hull married to a square sail and a keel, able to cross open water and then row straight onto any beach.

In 793 Norse raiders fell on Lindisfarne, killing monks, enslaving others, and stripping the church treasures of the spiritual heart of the Northumbrian golden age — a minor raid militarily and an earthquake culturally.

Alcuin, the Northumbrian scholar at Charlemagne's court and the most learned man of his generation, wrote letters home in horror, calling it a terror never before seen in Britain and reading it as God's punishment for English sins.

The very next year, 794, raiders struck Wearmouth-Jarrow — Bede's own monastery — proving this was a systematic campaign rather than a freak event and closing the window of safety the whole book had traced.

The symmetry is almost unbearable: Germanic sea-raiders had come out of the North Sea in 450 to unmake Roman Britain, and now another wave of Germanic seafarers was coming out of the same sea against the country the first wave had built.
